Duration Project — Analysis of Unemployment Periods

Replication of Lalive, van Ours & Zweimuller (2006, ReStud)

M2 AMSE

2026-03-12

  • Q2 — Difference-in-Differences Estimation
  • Q3 — Kaplan-Meier Survival Functions
  • Q4 — Smoothed Hazard Rate Estimation
  • Q5 - Estimating the causal treatment effect in a PH model

Main goal

The objective of the project is to demonstrate why standard linear evaluation techniques fail when applied to duration data, and why survival analysis is mathematically required to isolate the true behavioral response to Unemployment Insurance (UI) reforms. We analyze the approach from Lalive et al. (2006) and focus on methodology rather than a full re-analysis of the results.

Libraries and Data Loading
library(foreign)      
library(tidyverse)
library(survival)
library(survminer)
library(KernSmooth)
library(stargazer)

udat <- read.dta("~/Documents/S2/duartions_models/project_exam/fi.dta")
Data Preparation
Cleaning
sum(is.na(udat))
## [1] 0
# Drop superfluous variables (166 → 134)
udat <- udat[, 1:134]
udat <- as_tibble(udat)

dim(udat)
## [1] 225821    134
Treatment Group Distribution
table(udat$type)
## 
## PBD and RR        PBD         RR    control 
##      21174      99404      32470      72773
Duration Truncation at 104 Weeks

Durations are right-truncated at 104 weeks: \(t^{104}_u = \min(t_u,\, 104)\).

udat <- udat %>%
  mutate(dur104 = ifelse(dur > 104, 104, dur))

The truncation is correctly implemented. It is required so that the means computed in the DiD are comparable to those in Table 4 of the paper.

Q2 — Difference-in-Differences Estimation

The difference-in-differences estimator isolates the causal treatment effect by subtracting the common time trend absorbed by the control group:

\[ \tilde{\Delta}_{DD} = \left(\bar{Y}^{A}_{T} - \bar{Y}^{B}_{T}\right) - \left(\bar{Y}^{A}_{C} - \bar{Y}^{B}_{C}\right) \]

where \(\bar{Y}^{B}_{T}\) and \(\bar{Y}^{A}_{T}\) are the average unemployment durations for the treated group before and after the reform, and \(\bar{Y}^{B}_{C}\), \(\bar{Y}^{A}_{C}\) are the corresponding quantities for the control group.

# the before/after mean matrix
didd_in_diff <- udat %>%
  group_by(type, after) %>%
  summarize(Y_bar = mean(dur104, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = after, values_from = Y_bar, names_prefix = "Period_") %>%
  rename(Before = Period_0, After = Period_1) %>%
  mutate(Difference = After - Before)

# the control group's time trend
control_diff <- didd_in_diff %>%
  filter(as.character(type) == "control") %>%
  pull(Difference)

# Compute causal DiD parameters
didd_in_diff <- didd_in_diff %>%
  mutate(DiD_Estimator = Difference - control_diff)

print(didd_in_diff)
## # A tibble: 4 × 5
##   type       Before After Difference DiD_Estimator
##   <fct>       <dbl> <dbl>      <dbl>         <dbl>
## 1 PBD and RR   18.5  22.7       4.25         3.08 
## 2 PBD          15.8  18.1       2.25         1.08 
## 3 RR           17.1  19.1       1.99         0.818
## 4 control      14.5  15.6       1.17         0

Empirical Findings

The unconditional DiD matrix establishes a naive econometric baseline to evaluate the 1989 Austrian UI reform. The control group exhibits a structural macroeconomic deterioration, with average unemployment duration increasing by 1.17 weeks independently of the policy intervention. Differencing out this secular drift isolates the unconditional average treatment effects: increasing the replacement rate (eRR) prolongs duration by 0.82 weeks, while extending the potential benefit duration (ePBD) adds 1.08 weeks.

Crucially, the joint treatment effect (ePBD + RR) of 3.08 weeks strictly exceeds the linear sum of the isolated interventions (1.90 weeks). This provides preliminary empirical evidence of a non-linear, synergistic behavioral response.

Methodological Critique: Structural Limitations of OLS DiD on Duration Data

While the 2×2 DiD matrix proves the existence of a policy impact, applying standard linear estimators to raw duration data introduces severe econometric biases:

  • Right-Censoring Bias: Standard DiD estimators cannot process incomplete spells. Individuals who do not find a job before the observation window closes are recorded with truncated durations, artificially suppressing the variance and downwardly biasing the true magnitude of the policy impact.

  • Dynamic Sorting (Unobserved Heterogeneity): DiD on means assumes the policy impact is distributed equally across time and ignores the compositional shift of the unemployment pool. As workers with high unobserved employability exit the risk pool early, the remaining sample becomes increasingly dominated by individuals with intrinsically lower baseline hazards.

  • Theoretical Disconnect: Mortensen’s Job Search Theory dictates that worker behavior is not static — reservation wages decay and search intensity spikes dynamically as the benefit exhaustion point approaches. Condensing the entire spell into a single average duration metric obliterates these time-varying adjustments.

Q3 — Kaplan-Meier Survival Functions

# KM survival integrals and return a tidy data frame per group
compute_surv_integral <- function(dataset, target_type, group_label) {
  strata_data <- dataset %>% filter(as.character(type) == target_type)
  
  if (nrow(strata_data) == 0) {
    stop(sprintf("Error: 0 rows found for type '%s'. Check levels(udat$type).", target_type))
  }
  
  km_fit     <- survfit(Surv(dur, uncc) ~ after, data = strata_data)
  km_summary <- summary(km_fit, times = 0:104, extend = TRUE)
  
  surv_matrix <- data.frame(
    time   = km_summary$time,
    surv   = km_summary$surv,
    strata = km_summary$strata
  ) %>%
    mutate(strata = ifelse(grepl("after=0", strata), "S_before", "S_after")) %>%
    pivot_wider(names_from = strata, values_from = surv)
  
  expected_before <- sum(surv_matrix$S_before[-1], na.rm = TRUE)
  expected_after  <- sum(surv_matrix$S_after[-1],  na.rm = TRUE)
  delta_weeks     <- expected_after - expected_before
  
  surv_matrix$Group <- sprintf("%s (\u0394 = %+.2f weeks)", group_label, delta_weeks)
  return(surv_matrix)
}

# for all four groups
df_control <- compute_surv_integral(udat, "control",    "Control Group")
df_rr      <- compute_surv_integral(udat, "RR",         "eRR Group")
df_pbd     <- compute_surv_integral(udat, "PBD",        "ePBD Group")
df_pbdrr   <- compute_surv_integral(udat, "PBD and RR", "ePBD+RR Group")

df_all <- bind_rows(df_control, df_rr, df_pbd, df_pbdrr)

Geometric Decomposition of Expected Unemployment Duration

The results are close to the paper’s figures. The transition from unconditional DiD (Q2) to the topological decomposition of survivor functions \(\hat{S}(t)\) (Q3) is a strict econometric requirement to resolve the structural failures of linear models.

The Kaplan-Meier Estimator and Censoring

The Kaplan-Meier estimator is:

\[ \hat{S}(t) = \prod_{j\,:\,t_j \leq t} \left(1 - \frac{d_j}{n_j}\right) \]

where \(d_j\) is the number of exits and \(n_j\) the risk set size at time \(t_j\). Right-censored observations remain in the denominator up to their censoring point, yielding an unbiased step-wise survival function.

If we did that here, it was The transition from unconditional Difference-in-Differences (Q2) to the topological decomposition of survivor functions S^(t) (Q3) is a strict econometric requirement to resolve the structural failures of linear models. OLS regressions on raw duration data collapse the entire temporal distribution into a single scalar mean, introducing two fatal biases: the inability to process right-censored observations without distorting the variance, and the assumption that policy impacts are uniformly distributed across time. To map the true dynamic behavioral response predicted by Mortensen’s Job Search Theory, a non-parametric estimator is required to evaluate the exact temporal intervals driving the treatment effects.

The Kaplan-Meier Mechanic and Censoring Integration

The Kaplan-Meier (KM) estimator mathematically corrects the Q2 right-censoring bias by dynamically adjusting the risk set R(t). Individuals whose unemployment spells are truncated by the end of the observation window are not discarded or assigned artificially low durations; they remain in the denominator of the survival probability calculation up to their exact point of censoring. This preserves maximum statistical power and yields an unbiased, step-wise empirical survival function that maps the true probability of remaining unemployed past time t.

Topological Decomposition and Theoretical Mapping

The KM signatures validate the matrix architecture:

  • Control Group: The survivor function reveals a systemic upward drift post-week 20, confirming a structural labor market deterioration in Austria independent of the 1989 reform. This visualizes the absolute necessity of the DiD framework.

  • eRR Group (Income Effect): Demonstrates a continuous, time-invariant divergence starting immediately at \(t=0\). Higher unconditional cash flow permanently elevates the reservation wage without altering the exhaustion timeline — the survival curve exhibits a parallel shift, consistent with the proportional hazards assumption.

  • ePBD Group (Temporal Arbitrage): Exhibits a structural “lens effect” localized between weeks 15 and 45. The pre-reform exit spike at week 30 is delayed, demonstrating temporal arbitrage — workers shift the timing of their reservation wage adjustments to extract maximum entitlements. This mathematically rejects the proportional hazards assumption for this intervention.

  • ePBD+RR Group (Synergistic Superposition): Displays a compound signature: an immediate baseline divergence (income effect) amplified by a massive structural lens spanning weeks 15 to 65 (duration effect), accounting for the non-linear +3.08 week synergistic treatment effect.

Q4 — Smoothed Hazard Rate Estimation

The hazard rate (exit rate) is defined as:

\[ \lambda(t) = -\frac{d}{dt}\ln S(t) = \frac{f(t)}{S(t)} \]

The discrete approximation from the KM estimator is:

\[ \hat{\lambda}(t) = \frac{\hat{S}(t-1) - \hat{S}(t)}{\hat{S}(t-1)} \]

Raw step-function hazards are highly erratic. Local polynomial regression (degree 1) smooths them by minimising:

\[ \min_{a,b} \sum_i \left[Y_i - a - b(t_i - t)\right]^2 K_h(t_i - t) \]

Degree 1 (Local Linear) is preferred over degree 0 (Nadaraya-Watson) because it reduces boundary bias. Where NW tends to underestimate the hazard due toa lack symmetric informations aournd the estimation point.

Smoothed Exit Hazard Rates by Group and Reform Period

The results closely replicate Figure 4 of the paper.

Methodological Objective

While the Kaplan-Meier estimator S(t) (Q3) maps the cumulative probability of remaining unemployed, it obscures instantaneous behavioral adjustments. To quantify exact search intensity, the analysis transitions to the continuous Hazard Function λ(t)=−dtd​lnS(t). Raw empirical hazards derived from discrete step-functions yield non-differentiable noise. Applying local polynomial kernel regression filters this variance, isolating the true structural peaks of the exit rate to empirically test Mortensen’s Job Search Theory.

Empirical Findings and Theoretical Mapping

  • Income Effect Validation (eRR Group): The smoothed hazard exhibits a uniform downward translation across the duration domain. The exhaustion peak remains anchored at week 30. This confirms that higher cash flow permanently elevates the reservation wage without altering exit timing.

  • Temporal Arbitrage (ePBD Group): The pre-reform hazard spike at week 30 is eradicated and shifted to match the new administrative limits (week 39 or 52). This demonstrates that duration increases are driven by pure temporal arbitrage, not structural human capital decay.

Q5 - Estimating the causal treatment effect in a PH model

Justification for Q5: Estimating the Causal Treatment Effect in a Piecewise PH Model

While the Kaplan-Meier and smoothed hazard plots (Q3 and Q4) provide visual proof of temporal arbitrage and income effects, they are non-parametric tools. They cannot isolate the exact multivariate causal impact of the UI reform because they do not control for the macroeconomic drift or individual characteristics simultaneously. To extract the true causal parameters (δ), we must transition to a formal survival regression framework.

The transition to the Piecewise Exponential (PWE) Proportional Hazards architecture is a strict econometric requirement to resolve the structural misspecification inherent in standard continuous-time models (e.g., Weibull, Gompertz). The non-parametric diagnostics established in Q3 and Q4 proved that the baseline hazard \(\lambda_0(t)\) is highly non-monotonic, exhibiting discontinuous spikes driven by administrative benefit exhaustion. The PWE framework accommodates this temporal volatility by partitioning the time axis into discrete 4-week segments. Within each interval, the baseline hazard is assumed constant, but it is permitted to shift flexibly between adjacent intervals, computing a step-function that exactly maps the true empirical hazard geometry.

Baseline Hazard Absorption: By assigning an independent intercept parameter to each discrete interval, the PWE model explicitly absorbs the natural non-linear volatility of the exit rate—specifically the massive structural spike at week 30. This prevents the dynamic sorting and temporal clustering observed in the raw duration data from contaminating the regression estimators.

Causal Parameter Isolation: Once the macroeconomic drift and the highly non-linear baseline hazard (\(\lambda_0(t)\)) are structurally absorbed by the interval matrix, the model successfully isolates the Difference-in-Differences interaction terms (\(\delta\)). This architecture guarantees that the extracted \(\delta\) parameters represent the pure, uncontaminated behavioral disincentive effect induced by the UI extensions, rather than artifacts of baseline unobserved heterogeneity.

Continuous-Time Precision via Poisson Link: By expanding the longitudinal risk set via episode splitting and evaluating it through a Poisson Generalized Linear Model, the PWE architecture mathematically accounts for exact fractional survival within any given 4-week interval using the offset(log(exposure)) term. This mechanism averts the severe discretization bias inherent in grouped-time probability models (e.g., standard Logit or Complementary Log-Log), preserving the exact continuous-time precision required to rigorously test Mortensen’s Job Search Theory.

library(dplyr)
library(survival)

# 1. RAM Contingency: Isolate a 20% random sample from the original matrix
# Set a seed to guarantee exact reproducibility of your results
set.seed(42) 
udat_sample <- udat %>% sample_frac(0.20)

# 2. Re-calculate the control variables on the sampled dataframe
udat_sample <- udat_sample %>%
  mutate(all = tr * (t39 + t52))

# 3. Execute the Episode Splitting (SurvSplit) strictly on the smaller sample
breaks <- seq(from=3, to=59, by=4)
labels <- paste("(", c(0,breaks), ",", c(breaks,104), "]", sep="")

gux_sample <- survSplit(Surv(dur104, uncc) ~ ., data = udat_sample, 
                        cut = breaks, end = "time", event = "death", 
                        start = "start", episode = "interval")

gux_sample <- gux_sample %>%
  mutate(exposure = time - start,
         interval = factor(interval + 1, labels = labels))

# 4. Execute the fully interacted PWE model
# This will now process ~200,000 rows instead of 1,000,000, fitting easily into 16GB RAM
pwe_final_sampled <- glm(death ~ 
                           age + single + married + divorced + 
                           sfrau + f_marr + f_single + f_divor +
                           lehre + med_educ + hi_educ + ein_zus +
                           bc + lwage + ten72 + pnon_10 + seasonal + manuf +
                           y1988 + y1989 + y1990 + y1991 + q2 + q3 + q4 +
                           
                           interval +
                           interval:tr + interval:t39 + interval:t52 +
                           interval:t39_tr + interval:t52_tr +
                           interval:after +
                           
                           interval:tr:after + interval:t39:after +
                           interval:t52:after + interval:t39_tr:after +
                           interval:t52_tr:after +
                           
                           offset(log(exposure)),  
                         family = poisson(link = "log"), 
                         data = gux_sample)


library(stargazer)

stargazer(pwe_final_sampled, 
          dep.var.caption = "", dep.var.labels = "",
          keep = c("t52:after"),  
          omit.table.layout = "n", star.cutoffs = NA,
          keep.stat = c("n", "ll"), no.space = TRUE,
          header = FALSE,
          title = "The PWE Model: Time-Varying Causal Parameters (eP52 Group)", 
          type = "text")
## 
## The PWE Model: Time-Varying Causal Parameters (eP52 Group)
## =======================================
##                                        
## ---------------------------------------
## interval(0,3]:t52:after       0.056

## interval(3,7]:t52:after       -0.083

## interval(7,11]:t52:after      0.034    
##                              (0.073)   
## interval(11,15]:t52:after     -0.169   
##                              (0.079)   
## interval(15,19]:t52:after     -0.106   
##                              (0.101)   
## interval(19,23]:t52:after     -0.635   
##                              (0.139)   
## interval(23,27]:t52:after     -0.537   
##                              (0.184)   
## interval(27,31]:t52:after     -0.716   
##                              (0.208)   
## interval(31,35]:t52:after     -0.300   
##                              (0.257)   
## interval(35,39]:t52:after     -0.727   
##                              (0.321)   
## interval(39,43]:t52:after     -0.333   
##                              (0.377)   
## interval(43,47]:t52:after     0.996    
##                              (0.436)

## interval(51,55]:t52:after     1.406    
##                              (0.375)
